abstract:
In this study, we evaluate the potential of diffusion probabilistic models for generating high-quality three-dimensional (3D) medical data, such as CT and MRI scans. While generative models have seen significant success in 2D computer vision, their systematic application to 3D medical volumes remains limited despite their promise for privacy-preserving data sharing and dataset augmentation. 

Our contributions are fivefold: (i) We propose a novel architecture that generates high-resolution 3D images by learning the underlying structure of a VQ-GAN latent space, significantly reducing the computational overhead typically associated with 3D diffusion. (ii) We demonstrate the versatility of this method across four public datasets covering diverse modalities (CT and MRI) and anatomical regions (brain, chest, breast, and knee), maintaining performance even with small sample sizes (n ≤ 1844). (iii) We show that our model outperforms established 3D GAN benchmarks by producing more diverse images and avoiding the common pitfall of mode collapse. (iv) Recognizing that standard automated metrics often fail to capture clinical nuances, we validated our results through a rigorous qualitative assessment by experienced radiologists, who confirmed the realism and anatomical consistency of the generated volumes. (v) Finally, we demonstrate the practical utility of our synthetic data in low-data regimes. By pre-training a Swin-UNETR model in a self-supervised manner on synthetic images from one institution, we achieved a substantial performance boost in downstream segmentation tasks at other institutions. For instance, in breast MRI segmentation with only 5% available labeled data, Dice scores improved from 0.91 to 0.95. These results suggest that latent diffusion models are a powerful tool for overcoming data scarcity in medical imaging. We have made our code publicly available to facilitate further research in the field.
